FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, N=10,588 participants at 2-year follow-up assessment

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership (yes/no) and age of first smartphone acquisition measured via caregiver report; qualifies under 'ownership, age of acquisition' from FT-IC2 criteria

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects logistic regression models testing associations between smartphone ownership/acquisition and three non-SM health outcomes (depression, obesity, insufficient sleep)

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports ORs with 95% CIs for all associations; e.g., smartphone ownership vs. non-ownership: depression OR 1.31 (95% CI 1.05-1.63), obesity OR 1.40 (95% CI 1.20-1.63), insufficient sleep OR 1.62 (95% CI 1.46-1.79)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD data; cross-sectional analysis at age 12 (Analysis 1), among smartphone owners at age 12 (Analysis 2), and prospective analysis of new acquisition (Analysis 3)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Pediatrics, 2026; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone is the IV, not the DV. Paper tests smartphone ownership/acquisition as predictors of health outcomes (depression, obesity, sleep), not the reverse. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria.
notes: Smartphone ownership is a qualifying exposure per E13. All three analyses include smartphone/SM as independent variable predicting non-SM outcomes. Analysis 1 and 2 are cross-sectional; Analysis 3 is prospective but with non-SM outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,875 children at baseline (age 10), 1-year and 2-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Measured 'duration of screen use (distinguishing between social media, social engagement, passive watching, and active gaming)' as one of multiple lifestyle factors. This is generic 'total screen time' or composite without phone/SM-specific coefficient extractable separately. Per FT-IC2 criteria: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET. Paper does not isolate a specific smartphone or social media variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cannot fully assess from available pages. Paper tests associations between neurodevelopmental spectrum (age 10) and psychiatric outcomes (ages 11-12), with lifestyle factors as mediators. Screen use appears to be a mediator variable, not a standalone exposure tested for association with non-SM outcome.

FT_IC4.evidence: Not yet determined; paper does report β coefficients for mediation models but unclear if screen use has separately reported coefficient as primary exposure

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using linear mixed models and mediation models; longitudinal design from ages 10-12

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAACAP Open, 2026; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen use is not the primary focus or standalone exposure. It is measured as a mediator in the pathway between neurodevelopmental traits (IV) and psychiatric outcomes (DV). FT-IC2 failure due to generic screen time composite.
notes: EXCLUSION via FT-IC2: Screen use is measured as generic 'duration' without phone/SM-specific breakdown. Paper is about neurodevelopmental pathways and psychiatric outcomes, with lifestyle factors (including screen use) as candidate mediators. Does not qualify as a smartphone/social media focused paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, N=10,075 at baseline (age 9-10)

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract reports 'self-reported screen time' as lifestyle behavior exposure. This is generic screen time without specification of smartphone or social media component. Per FT-IC2: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET. No PDF available to verify if breakdown exists.

FT_IC3.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract indicates multinomial logistic regression to examine associations between baseline lifestyle behaviors (screen time, physical activity, caffeine, sleep, recreational activities) and PLE trajectories, which suggests FT-IC3 MET, but cannot confirm without full text.

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract reports ORs for screen time: 'adjusted Odds Ratio [OR] 2.27, 95% confidence interval [CI] 2.03-2.53' for Persistent Elevated Distress vs. No Distress trajectory. If screen time coefficient is reported, FT-IC4 MET, but cannot verify without PDF.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using latent growth mixture models and multinomial logistic regressions; longitudinal design identifying PLE trajectories

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 2026; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time appears to be an IV predicting PLE trajectories (DV), not the reverse. However, FT-IC2 NOT_MET due to generic screen time without phone/SM-specific breakdown.
notes: EXCLUSION via FT-IC2: Paper measured 'self-reported screen time' as a generic lifestyle factor without distinguishing smartphone or social media use. Per criteria, generic total screen time without phone/SM breakdown does not qualify. No PDF available to verify if more specific breakdown exists in full text.
